Zophar strongly criticized Job. He portrayed him as a man who enjoyed hearing himself speak, even though he had nothing meaningful to say, and as someone who defended falsehoods. Zophar hoped that God would show Job that the punishment he received was less than what he deserved. We are quick to call on God to settle our disagreements and believe that if he were to speak, he would support us. We should leave all disputes to God’s judgment, which we know is true; but those who are most eager to appeal to divine judgment are not always correct.

Zophar speaks well about God and his greatness and glory, about people and their emptiness and foolishness. Observe what people are like; let them be humbled. God sees that people want to be wise and appear to be so, even though they are born like a wild ass’s colt – untrainable and unmanageable. People are vain; empty, as the word says. Yet they are proud and self-important. They want to be wise and seem to be so, but they will not submit to wisdom's principles. They want to be wise, they seek forbidden knowledge, and like our first parents, trying to be wiser than what is written, they lose eternal life for the sake of knowledge. Is a creature like this fit to argue with God?

Zophar urges Job to repent and offers him encouragement, though mixed with harsh opinions. He believed that worldly prosperity always belongs to the righteous, and that Job should be considered a hypocrite unless his fortune was restored. Then you will lift up your face without shame; that is, you may approach the throne of grace boldly, without terror or amazement as expressed in chapter [reference omitted]. If we are seen in the presence of Christ, our faces, which were cast down, may be lifted up; though they were defiled, now washed with the blood of Christ, they may be lifted up without shame. We may draw near with full assurance of faith when we are cleansed from an evil conscience.
